content.claim: School choice patterns in Finland have been associated with growing residential and school segregation through middle-class choice behavior, replicating some selection-like effects within a formally non-tracking system, though scholarly interpretation differs on whether this represents genuine 'erosion' of the comprehensive ideal or resilient adaptation.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Debate is genuinely contested: some analysts attribute segregation to neoliberal erosion, others interpret selective classes as evidence of model resilience—different causal narratives from same phenomena
Magnitude of choice-driven segregation relative to residential sorting not decomposed in available evidence
Counterfactual (what comprehensiveness without choice would look like) not observable
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
